title: Chapter 4: The Fine Print
content: Three days in the penthouse and it still looks like a hotel room I'm squatting in.

Floor-to-ceiling windows frame the Manhattan skyline like it's a screensaver — the kind that comes pre-installed on a laptop you can't afford. The furniture is all matte white and brushed steel, everything selected by some algorithm that knew my aesthetic before I did. Mid-century modern couch I've never sat on. A kitchen island the size of my old bathroom. Zero tchotchkes, zero personality, zero evidence that anyone actually lives here.
Including me.

I'm sprawled on the couch — okay, I sat on it once, sue me — scrolling through my feed. My notifications are a firehose. Brand deals, collab requests, people I went to high school with suddenly in my DMs acting like we were close. The algorithm loves me. The algorithm has loved me since that night I pressed "I Agree" on the Mephisto MCN contract, and the love has been relentless, suffocating, exactly the kind of attention I used to dream about when I was eating ramen in a studio with a leaking faucet.
My thumb pauses on a comment from some girl with an anime pfp: "chloe used to care about stuff. now she's just another content machine."
I scroll past it. My pulse doesn't even tick up. Weird. A month ago, that would've sent me spiraling for an hour.
The doorbell rings.

I didn't order anything. I didn't invite anyone. I don't even know where the doorbell is — the intercom is this sleek black panel by the entrance that I've never used. I stare at it like it's going to explain itself.
It rings again.

I get up, pad across the heated marble floor — heated, because of course, this place has floors that cost more than my mother's house — and press the intercom button. Nothing. No camera feed, no voice. Just silence and then a third ring, long and low, like a church bell somehow compressed into a doorbell chime.
I open the door.

He's not a delivery guy. He's not a fan. He's standing in my hallway like he was painted there — a man in a suit that probably costs more than my entire old apartment building. Late thirties, though something about his face resists aging the way marble resists weather. Dark eyes. Not brown-dark. Dark-dark. The kind of dark that doesn't reflect light. He's smiling, but the smile stops two inches below his eyes, which are busy doing something else entirely. Cataloguing. Measuring.
"Chloe Hart." It's not a question. His voice is warm, unhurried, the voice of someone who has never once been told to speed up.
"Who are you?"
"Mephisto." He extends his hand. "Just Mephisto. Like Cher."

I don't take his hand. The name lands somewhere in the back of my skull with a dull thunk — I've seen it before, on the logo, on the welcome emails, on the contract header. Mephisto MCN. The CEO. Standing in my hallway at — I check the clock on the wall — 7:43 PM on a Tuesday.

He doesn't wait for me to invite him in. He just walks past me, and the cologne hits me like a wall — oud and vetiver and something underneath that I can only describe as the smell of old libraries, the smell of paper so old the acid is eating it from the inside out. It's expensive. It's suffocating. It's the kind of scent that doesn't ask permission to enter your lungs.

He's already surveying the penthouse. Head on a swivel, nodding approvingly, like a landlord doing an inspection. He runs a finger along the kitchen island — matte black marble, I didn't pick it — and examines the tip of his finger for dust. There isn't any. He nods again.
"The algorithm chose well," he says. "You have excellent bones."
"Excuse me?"

"The apartment." He gestures broadly. "Good flow. Natural light. Proximity to water towers — we've found they anchor the signal surprisingly well. And the bones of the building, the iron skeleton. Very conductive." He says this like he's discussing feng shui. "But you —" He turns to me, and those dark eyes refocus with the precision of a microscope clicking into place. "You also have excellent bones. Metaphorically speaking. The algorithm prioritizes creators with high emotional density. You're practically humming with it."
"Thanks," I say, because I don't know what else to say. "I moisturize."
His smile widens by a millimeter. "I can see why you were selected."

He sits down on the white couch — sits on my couch, in my apartment, that I've barely sat on — and pulls an iPad from inside his jacket. The jacket is charcoal, nearly black, and the fabric has a sheen that shifts between matte and silk depending on the angle. It's not from any designer I recognize. It might not be from any designer at all.

"Shall we get to business?" He taps the iPad awake. "I'm here to congratulate you, Chloe. You've hit your first emotional milestone — one million followers. The empathy deduction has been processed successfully. You may have noticed."
My stomach drops. Not flips — drops, like an elevator whose cable just snapped.
"I noticed," I manage.

"Wonderful. Customer awareness is very important to us." He swipes on the iPad. "However, you appear to have missed your five-million-follower notification. Trust was deducted forty-seven hours ago. I'm here to walk you through the terms and ensure you're... onboarding smoothly."
"Trust," I repeat. The word feels foreign. Like I'm learning it for the first time. "You deducted trust."

"Per your contract." He turns the iPad toward me. The screen shows a document so dense it looks like someone tried to compress the entire IRS tax code into a single page. He zooms in. Page 47, Section 12(d), the text small and clinical:
"Creator acknowledges and agrees that emotional milestones (1M, 5M, 10M, 25M, 50M) trigger automatic deduction of designated emotional capacities as specified in Schedule C."
My eyes drift down. Schedule C is a table, neat as a spreadsheet:
1M — Empathy
5M — Trust
10M — Grief
25M — Nostalgia
50M — Love
Five milestones. Five emotions. Ticked off like items on a grocery list.
"That's —" My voice cracks. I clear my throat. "That's insane. That can't be legal. I didn't — I didn't read the contract. Nobody reads the contract."

Mephisto tilts his head. The gesture is birdlike, predatory, the way an owl processes a sound in the dark. "Nobody reads the contract," he repeats, tasting each word. "That's true. Statistically, less than zero-point-one percent of our creators read beyond page three. But you know what every single one of them does?"
I don't answer.

He swipes to page one of the iPad and turns it toward me. There it is. The checkbox. The little square I tapped at 2:47 AM in my shithole apartment while my phone buzzed with my first thousand notifications. The box that says, in clear, 8-point font: **I have read and agree to the Terms of Service.**
"You checked the box, Chloe." His voice is gentle. That's the worst part. It's so gentle. "Your digital signature is on file. Your blood, digitized, is embedded in the contract."
"My — what?"
He zooms into the margin of page one. There, almost invisible — a thin red thread, woven into the digital paper like a watermark. It pulses faintly, rhythmically, like a capillary.

"Your DNA," he says. "Collected via the fingerprint authentication during signup. Encoded and woven into the contract fiber. It's how we verify the binding. It's also how we know — with absolute certainty — that it was you." He taps the red thread, and it blinks, and I swear I feel my own pulse stutter in response, a sympathetic vibration, like a tuning fork pressed against my sternum.
I want to throw up. The taste rises in my throat — sour, metallic, the taste of fear concentrated into bile. I swallow it down.
"So what," I say, and my voice is doing its best impression of someone who has her shit together, "I just lose pieces of myself every time I get more famous? That's the business model?"
"That is the business model." He says it without a flicker of shame. "Think of it as a subscription. You subscribed to success, and emotions are the monthly fee. Very standard."

"Very standard," I echo. My laugh comes out wrong — sharp, too high, the kind of laugh that happens right before crying. "Okay. Cool. So what happens if I cancel my subscription? Delete my account? Walk away?"
He looks at me the way you'd look at a child who just asked what happens to the hamburger after you eat it. Then he swipes to a new page. Section 89.
"Early termination clause," he reads. "Penalty: full and immediate forfeiture of the Creator's life essence." He glances up. "In lay terms? You die."
The word sits between us like a grenade with the pin pulled.
"You die," I repeat.

"You die," he confirms, and he says it gently, like he's delivering bad news about a flight cancellation. Gate changed. Your connection is missed. Your life is forfeit. Please check the departure board for updates.

"Okay." I press my palms flat against the kitchen island. The marble is cold and real and my hands are shaking and I need something cold and real. "Okay. So I'm trapped in a contract I didn't read that's literally harvesting my emotions and if I try to leave I die. Is that — is that the summary? Because that sounds like the summary."

"That is, essentially, the summary." He reaches into his jacket and produces a business card. Black cardstock, gold lettering, just one word: MEPHISTO. He places it on the counter between us like he's laying down a trump card. "For future reference. My direct line. I'm always available to our creators."
I stare at the card. Then I pick it up and drop it into the trash can under the counter.
The card burns my fingers.

Not metaphorically. Actually burns — a quick, searing kiss of heat against my thumb and index finger. I yelp and shake my hand, and when I look down at the counter, the card is back. Sitting where he placed it. Gold lettering gleaming. Not a scorch mark on it.
"The card doesn't go in the trash," Mephisto says. "Contractually speaking, it remains in your possession at all times. Section 91, if you're curious."

I'm not curious. I'm not curious about anything in this contract ever again because curiosity is how I got here — curious about what it would feel like to be seen, to be followed, to matter. Look where curiosity got me.

I turn away from him. My heartbeat is so loud I can hear it in my teeth, a thick, wet rhythm, the sound of my body trying to remind me it's still here, still alive, still mine — though for how much longer, and how much of me will be left, are both open questions.

Mephisto stands. He smooths the front of his jacket — that impossible fabric shifting between coal and midnight — and crosses to the door. His heels don't make a sound on the marble. He moves like smoke given intention.
At the door, he turns.

"You're taking the deductions remarkably well, by the way," he says. "Most creators are catatonic by the third milestone. Non-responsive. We had one who just sat in her apartment and screamed for six weeks. Very inconvenient for the building." He pauses. His eyes flicker — and I don't know what it is, amusement or interest or hunger, but it's something, and it's directed at me with the focus of a magnifying glass on an ant. "You're something of an outlier, Chloe. I'll be watching."
The door closes behind him. Not a slam. Not a click. It just closes the way a mouth closes around a swallow.
I'm alone.

The iPad is still on the counter, the contract still glowing on its screen. My fingers are shaking and the burn on my thumb is already blistering, a small, perfect oval where the card kissed me. I pick up the iPad — slowly, like it might bite — and I scroll.
I scroll past Section 89, past the early termination clause, past the penalties and the subclauses and the definitions and the wall of text that ate my life. I scroll to the very last page. Page 100.
There, at the bottom, in font so small I have to pinch-zoom until my screen is just a few square inches of gray text, is a single paragraph:
*"Governing Law: Uniform Commercial Code, 1923 Edition. Jurisdiction: Infernal Court."*

Infernal Court. The word "infernal" tickles something in my brain — some half-remembered vocab lesson, some footnote from a literature class I slept through. But it's the year that sticks. 1923. Not 2023. Not any year that makes sense for a tech company's contract. 1923. Like the contract was written before the internet, before smartphones, before any of this was possible.
Like it was written before I was born. Before my mother was born. Before anything I recognize as my world even existed.
The year lodges in my brain like a splinter. Under the skin. Too deep to pull out with fingers.

I set the iPad down. The penthouse is silent. The Manhattan skyline glitters through the floor-to-ceiling windows like it's mocking me — all that light, all that life, and me on the wrong side of the glass. The black business card gleams on the counter. The blister on my thumb throbs. My heartbeat fills the apartment like a second tenant.
I have lost empathy. I have lost trust. At ten million, I'll lose grief. At twenty-five million, nostalgia. And at fifty million — love.
The worst part isn't that I signed it.
The worst part is that the algorithm is so, so good at its job, and my follower count is climbing by the second, and I can't make it stop.
I never could.
